results.title: Accel invests in Generation Lab, a longevity startup. - LinkedIn
results.content:
Adaptyx Biosciences just raised a $14M seed round, and it’s not another wearable chasing vanity metrics. This Menlo Park crew is decoding the body’s molecular language in real time: no guesswork, no gimmicks, just pure #biochemical signal. The round, led by Interlagos with Overwater Ventures, Starbloom Capital, Stanford University, Chan Zuckerberg Biohub Network, Hyperlink Ventures, Cantos, Humba Ventures, and Seaside Equity Partners, pushes total funding to $23M since 2022. That’s not luck, it’s physics, chemistry, and 17 yrs of Stanford & CZ Biohub research finally moving out of the lab and onto your skin. Co-founders Vijit Sabnis (CEO), Alex Yoshikawa (CSO), and Pawan Kapur (CTO) built Adaptyx on molecular intelligence that reads the body’s real-time #chemistry like Spotify reads [...] Let's talk about what happens when science stops chasing youth and starts quantifying it. Generation Lab just raised $11M in seed funding, led by Accel, marking Accel's 1st-ever longevity bet. Not a small move. Accel doesn't throw darts at startup boards. They play chess, and they just made a bold opening move into the biotech future of staying younger, longer. Founded by Alina Rui Su, Dr. Irina Conboy, and Michael Suswal, Generation Lab isn't just talking about #aging; they're measuring it at the molecular level. Alina Rui Su, Harvard Medical School PhD, University of California, Berkeley bioengineering alum, Forbes 30 Under 30, and someone who filed her 1st medical patent at 15, teamed up with Dr. Irina Conboy, the UC Berkeley professor whose #research on plasma dilution flipped [...] 200   25 Comments

# Accel invests in Generation Lab, a longevity startup.

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[Delphine Le Grand](https://www.linkedin.com/in/delphinelegrandofficial?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

* [Report this post](/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fdelphinelegrandofficial_accels-first-longevity-bet-generation-activity-7389309878919626753-B2CJ&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

[Accel](https://www.linkedin.com/company/accel-vc?trk=public_post-text)'s first longevity bet? [Generation Lab](https://www.linkedin.com/company/generation-lab-longevity?trk=public_post-text) and its $11M seed raise. Last week, one of Silicon Valley’s top venture firms made it official, Accel is going all in on longevity with Gen Lab. Their goal? To make measuring how fast we’re aging as routine as "measuring cholesterol". They've already partnered with more than 300 clinics (and as someone who gets asked all the time how to get access to clinics, trust me this is no small feat), offering tools that help physicians quantify biological aging through blood, epigenetic, and phenotypic data. But the real play goes beyond tests, it’s about the data infrastructure for aging itself. → 𝗔 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 Most diagnostic data sits in silos. Gen Lab is building the connective tissue that links biomarker testing, patient data, and predictive models. That unlocks a new category of longitudinal insights, tracking aging like we track disease. → 𝗟𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗴𝗼𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 What started with wearables and wellness labs is now entering medical practice. The next decade of longevity will combine software, data, and physician networks. → 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗲𝗹 When a firm known for backing facebook, spotify, dropbox makes its first longevity investment, it’s more than capital, it’s validation that this field has crossed the threshold from speculation to inevitability (let's gooooooo). TLDR - Generation Lab is bridging consumer curiosity and clinical credibility, and it’s especially inspiring to see women like [Alina Rui Su](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alinasu?trk=public_post-text) leading the charge in shaping what the future of longevity looks like. ♻️ Repost if you’re following the evolution of longevity diagnostics. Follow me at [Delphine Le Grand](https://www.linkedin.com/in/delphinelegrandofficial?trk=public_post-text) for more from the edge of health, science, and innovation.

Love this move—and agree the real moat is the data rail, not the single test. What I’m watching to separate signal from hype: -Standards & interoperability: FHIR-first pipes, harmonized biomarker ontologies, and provenance so multi-omics isn’t apples/oranges. -Clinical proof → reimbursement: prospective outcomes, CPT pathways, and cost-offset models (risk stratification that actually lowers spend). -Model governance: drift monitoring, transparent feature importance, and patient-level explanations clinicians trust. -Reference ranges that move: aging signals are longitudinal—does the platform recalibrate per cohort, sex, life stage, meds? -Data rights & portability: clean consent, revocation, and patient-owned vaults so clinics don’t feel locked-in and regulators don’t balk. If Gen Lab nails clinician workflow + outcomes + payer logic, longevity becomes a category—not a gadget. Excited to see Alina Rui Su push this across the credibility chasm.

  Let's talk about what happens when science stops chasing youth and starts quantifying it. [Generation Lab](https://www.linkedin.com/company/generation-lab-longevity?trk=public_post-text) just raised $11M in seed funding, led by [Accel](https://www.linkedin.com/company/accel-vc?trk=public_post-text), marking Accel's 1st-ever longevity bet. Not a small move. Accel doesn't throw darts at startup boards. They play chess, and they just made a bold opening move into the biotech future of staying younger, longer. Founded by [Alina Rui Su](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alinasu?trk=public_post-text), Dr. Irina Conboy, and [Michael Suswal](https://www.linkedin.com/in/michael-suswal-51b45421?trk=public_post-text), Generation Lab isn't just talking about [#aging](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Faging&trk=public_post-text); they're measuring it at the molecular level. Alina Rui Su, [Harvard Medical School](https://www.linkedin.com/school/harvard-medical-school/?trk=public_post-text) PhD, [University of California, Berkeley](https://www.linkedin.com/school/uc-berkeley/?trk=public_post-text) bioengineering alum, [Forbes](https://www.linkedin.com/company/forbes-magazine?trk=public_post-text) 30 Under 30, and someone who filed her 1st medical patent at 15, teamed up with Dr. Irina Conboy, the UC Berkeley professor whose [#research](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fresearch&trk=public_post-text) on plasma dilution flipped [#agingbiology](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fagingbiology&trk=public_post-text) on its head. Add Michael Suswal, co-founder of [Standard AI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/standard-ai?trk=public_post-text) and a man who knows how to scale systems from lab bench to market trench, and you've got a team built for impact. Their platform, SystemAge™, doesn't guess your [#biologicalage](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbiologicalage&trk=public_post-text), it measures it. With over 460 [#epigenetic](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fepigenetic&trk=public_post-text) markers across 19 organ systems, Generation Lab can tell you how old your [#brain](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbrain&trk=public_post-text), [#heart](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fheart&trk=public_post-text), [#liver](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fliver&trk=public_post-text), or [#immunesystem](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fimmunesystem&trk=public_post-text) really is. They call it "measuring biological noise." Translation: the closer your body is to static, the younger you are. When Ellen joined our Springboard program, she came from the lab driven by curiosity and conviction and not from a business background. In a few intense weeks, she learned to think like an investor: how to structure financing, evaluate cap tables, and align long-term strategy with fundraising. She turned that learning into action and in just a few years, Envue gets acquired by a German global leader in bioanalytical tools, [NanoTemper Technologies](https://de.linkedin.com/company/nanotemper-technologies-gmbh?trk=public_post-text). This isn’t a “lucky exit” or a “quick win”. It is the result of a founder who combined deep science with commercial clarity; a lean, disciplined approach to building. And an investor ecosystem that believed in her before the market did. For investors, Envue’s story is a reminder that Deep tech exits don’t behave like SaaS multiples. ✅They mature slowly, compound quietly, and then unlock disproportionate value once industry incumbents catch up. ✅The best returns often come from the least obvious founders. ✅Ecosystems matter. Networks accelerate readiness, confidence, and alignment. Ellen said it best: “You don’t need a perfect plan, just the courage to start. Be brave enough to fail, because that’s where you learn the most.” Today, Envue’s technology lives on powering discoveries in labs around the world. And for us at [Connect Sverige Region Väst](https://se.linkedin.com/company/connect-sverige-region-vast?trk=public_post-text), it’s proof that when science meets strategy, impact follows. The exits that look sudden from the outside are years of ecosystem discipline on the inside. To the investors reading this, keep backing the Ellens of the world. They’re building the future, one molecule at a time.

  [Adaptyx Biosciences](https://www.linkedin.com/company/adaptyx-biosciences?trk=public_post-text) just raised a $14M seed round, and it’s not another wearable chasing vanity metrics. This Menlo Park crew is decoding the body’s molecular language in real time: no guesswork, no gimmicks, just pure [#biochemical](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbiochemical&trk=public_post-text) signal. The round, led by [Interlagos](https://www.linkedin.com/company/interlagoscapital?trk=public_post-text) with [Overwater Ventures](https://www.linkedin.com/company/overwater-ventures?trk=public_post-text), [Starbloom Capital](https://www.linkedin.com/company/starbloom-capital?trk=public_post-text), [Stanford University](https://www.linkedin.com/school/stanford-university/?trk=public_post-text), [Chan Zuckerberg Biohub Network](https://www.linkedin.com/company/cz-biohub?trk=public_post-text), [Hyperlink Ventures](https://www.linkedin.com/company/hyperlinkventures?trk=public_post-text), [Cantos](https://www.linkedin.com/company/cantos-ventures?trk=public_post-text), [Humba Ventures](https://www.linkedin.com/company/humba-ventures?trk=public_post-text), and [Seaside Equity Partners](https://www.linkedin.com/company/seaside-equity-partners?trk=public_post-text), pushes total funding to $23M since 2022. That’s not luck, it’s physics, chemistry, and 17 yrs of Stanford & CZ Biohub research finally moving out of the lab and onto your skin. Co-founders [Vijit Sabnis](https://www.linkedin.com/in/vijitsabnis?trk=public_post-text) (CEO), [Alex Yoshikawa](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alex-yoshikawa?trk=public_post-text) (CSO), and [Pawan Kapur](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pawan-kapur-83723515?trk=public_post-text) (CTO) built Adaptyx on molecular intelligence that reads the body’s real-time [#chemistry](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fchemistry&trk=public_post-text) like Spotify reads playlists, continuously and personally. Sabnis, ex-Khosla Ventures partner and Solar Junction founder, knows how to turn science into industry. Yoshikawa earned his Ph.D. under [H. Tom Soh](https://www.linkedin.com/in/h-tom-soh-752224273?trk=public_post-text) at Stanford, while Kapur’s been the quiet hardware assassin behind Mojo Vision & Sunpreme. Add Professors Soh & [Joseph DeSimone](https://www.linkedin.com/in/josephdesimone1?trk=public_post-text), both Stanford heavyweights, and you’ve got a founding roster that could publish in Nature before breakfast. The tech hits different. Adaptyx’s platform uses programmable [#DNA](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdna&trk=public_post-text) switches that light up when analytes, glucose, potassium, lactate, cortisol, appear in your [#bloodstream](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbloodstream&trk=public_post-text). It’s a nightclub for molecules, with fluorescence instead of strobe lights. That signal becomes a continuous molecular feed, translating the chaos of biochemistry into a clear story about how your body’s actually doing. Forget snapshots. This is the movie. The implications are massive: continuous [#heartfailure](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fheartfailure&trk=public_post-text) monitoring, real-time [#hormone](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fhormone&trk=public_post-text) & [#metabolism](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fmetabolism&trk=public_post-text) tracking, and precision [#drugdosing](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdrugdosing&trk=public_post-text) that could redefine critical care. The prototype tracks five biomarkers, potassium, sodium, creatinine, urea, NT-proBNP, and next expands to hundreds more. Think of it as a molecular search engine for human health. Leading investors’ side, [Achal Upadhyaya](https://www.linkedin.com/in/achal-upadhyaya?trk=public_post-text) of Interlagos joins the board after a decade at SpaceX, because who better to scale precision from rockets to biowearables? [Kristina Simmons](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kristina-simmons-6964647?trk=public_post-text) of Overwater (ex-Khosla, a16z) brings the operational gravity that keeps startups from drifting. The capital fuels R&D, clinical trials, and growth across [#engineering](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fengineering&trk=public_post-text), [#datascience](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdatascience&trk=public_post-text) & [#clinicalops](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fclinicalops&trk=public_post-text), all aimed at [FDA](https://www.linkedin.com/company/fda?trk=public_post-text) clearance before a wellness rollout. Adaptyx isn’t chasing trends; it’s building infrastructure for how we’ll monitor health in the 2030s, a bridge between the quantified and clinically verified self. Continuous monitoring just got molecular. # **SCET Alum and Berkeley Professor Launch Generation Lab for Personalized Anti-Aging**

[![Berkeley Bioengineering Prof. Irina Conboy, Michael Suswal and SCET Alum and former Collider Cup winner, Alina Su](https://scet.berkeley.edu/wp-content/uploads/DSC02801-300x200.jpg)](https://scet.berkeley.edu/scet-alum-and-berkeley-professor-launch-generation-lab-for-personalized-anti-aging/ "SCET Alum and Berkeley Professor Launch Generation Lab for Personalized Anti-Aging")

**SCET Alum and [former Collider Cup](https://scet.berkeley.edu/collider-cup-profile-alina-su-ceo-founder-of-novaxs-biotech-corp-winner-of-pad-13-pitch-competition-member-of-berkeley-skydeck-program-and-1st-place-of-berkeley-fall-2021-collider-cup/) winner, Alina Su, and University of California, Berkeley Bioengineering Professor Irina Conboy are co-founding Generation Lab with the** **Mission to Extend the Human Healthspan – Waitlist for Its Clinically Driven At-Home Aging Test + Personalized Aging Intervention Opens Today**

[Generation Lab](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=TeZUXWpUv-2B6TCY38pVLo9nqCzlsziu-2F4wh5D2NlmVo9uL4RE6RyHrpPEhkFUxj8rHV6T_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioB280ksQbNBb8yCQth9i-2BjwifrcC0BGg1m5MgDd79GDbPPPLvnZ0Si1pKTGAmeTR4YNnjb7pC2XPr9vrCpxx74g41iDhTDaASDIJ58tCxW-2B8PR-2F0s1UKP7vYzbLO46-2FWZDR1hup1PFZ-2BrxMhh8XLxI-3D) will offer the first test based on biomedical evidence that measures an individual’s biological age progression, officially launched today out of stealth. The company is announcing its pre-seed funding and the opening of its [test kit waitlist](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=CVi7es-2FCNK-2FU4f58lHsDTlDj1TlDMlIK48xsd3z0C-2BA-3DZoqj_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4Joiuwcyerl-2FsPn4Y6mPgzeyYHHmKvmQ7w0dDw-2B3zOdFAPJHnr9b7kUXavh7Sp2xwWAmEd0CR9OZgw0fX7HOd7TcYf0-2BlAM9tMwGgVJQ94iTVwjJTFV-2B1hFCrJ0eHwKfZMK7MasE-2F9pLVZplCiGQhkNuy4-3D).

Co-founded by [Irina Conboy](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATUU2LHKSbPg854wTzRTSv0e9PJ582xD6DNSESk2gn6QZaexS60B0sE2Zi9kyeK2jSBQ-3D-3DBHg5_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioJD040nHobNbpWz3V5eSPxVO0LZnsm0kHydeleYuGZWPAqLGD-2FWNnuMi78Y3hx-2FJJs7syIuUdGHZgTzLq0IeYHUkTq3kHAojLc41S7LnQF6xFlpjM1CvwfSNSR66Uv5gq7prLU1SDZd7kqOHJAnDKs-3D), a preeminent aging and longevity researcher from UC Berkeley, Generation Lab combines a simple cheek-swab test with proprietary techniques to definitively measure molecular disbalance that indicates aging and risk of disease. The company works with clinicians who can recommend personalized interventions, and Generation Lab can measure the effectiveness of these over time.

According to the [CDC](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATURHuYuDkY-2F8kFuqzju5-2BdQf-2BplA0M-2BpvnD5M2MUQMvg8jjSButV23RpULQLJ66ySbw-3D-3DedM0_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioyR5HW0MF-2F2k3wNtm38leZlFS8yrqfiu3ULrrxw8OXqO-2B-2FZRj7Zcq7E8zOrI9yqVhDXhwF7MbNUczj9zCLnhvMv0yj1ULZBvLvu2NQFYWgL-2B36K94LDfNISp4AuDz5ggr5YCIh28H2vxG1TnuAhq6M-3D), six in ten adults in the US have at least one chronic disease and four in ten adults have two or more. Many of these are correlated with aging. These diseases are a major cause of morbidity and mortality and place a significant burden on our healthcare system. Catching these conditions early and preventing their progression can save millions of lives and billions of dollars. That’s what Generation Lab plans to do.

Unlike biological clocks, which are based on linear models that predict aging and disease by comparing a few people to each other, Generation Lab quantifies the actual biological age of each person through precise measurement of molecular biological noise – a key barometer of aging. Moreover, the biological age is quantified by Generation Lab for each function of the body: inflammation, regeneration, homeostasis, reproduction, etc.  Generation Lab’s solution is based on peer-reviewed science – including groundbreaking research led by Dr. Irina Conboy, UC Berkeley, and published as a cover story in Aging in Sept 2023.

“Certain molecular disbalance causes degenerative diseases that flare up with aging,” explained Dr. Conboy, co-founder of Generation Lab, and Professor at University of California at Berkeley. “Generation Lab’s tests enable us to identify when a person has a proclivity towards certain conditions and suggest approaches that can help to attenuate, delay or even eliminate those risks. This paves the way to novel anti-aging medicine for identifying and treating diseases early – even when a person is pre-symptomatic – which leads to better outcomes.”

Here’s how Generation Lab works:

* Obtain a test kit from a physician, clinic, or from Generation Lab directly.
* Open the kit and scan the QR code, selecting your level of membership (entry-level subscriptions start at about $400 per year for three tests).
* Follow the directions for a cheek swab and mail the sample back to Generation Lab in a pre-addressed envelope or return it to the clinician who provided the test.
* Within a few weeks, Generation Lab will provide a personalized report highlighting the status of your specific molecular aging markers, and offering a virtual call with a physician to review clinical and lifestyle interventions you might consider to slow the aging process or improve health.
* After subsequent Generation Lab tests, your clinician can review how effective each intervention was and its impact on your health and rate of aging.

According to the [CDC](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATURHuYuDkY-2F8kFuqzju5-2BdQf-2BplA0M-2BpvnD5M2MUQMvg8jjSButV23RpULQLJ66ySbw-3D-3DedM0_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioyR5HW0MF-2F2k3wNtm38leZlFS8yrqfiu3ULrrxw8OXqO-2B-2FZRj7Zcq7E8zOrI9yqVhDXhwF7MbNUczj9zCLnhvMv0yj1ULZBvLvu2NQFYWgL-2B36K94LDfNISp4AuDz5ggr5YCIh28H2vxG1TnuAhq6M-3D), six in ten adults in the US have at least one chronic disease and four in ten adults have two or more. Many of these are correlated with aging. These diseases are a major cause of morbidity and mortality and place a significant burden on our healthcare system. Catching these conditions early and preventing their progression can save millions of lives and billions of dollars. That’s what Generation Lab plans to do.

Unlike biological clocks, which are based on linear models that predict aging and disease by comparing a few people to each other, Generation Lab quantifies the actual biological age of each person through precise measurement of molecular biological noise – a key barometer of aging. Moreover, the biological age is quantified by Generation Lab for each function of the body: inflammation, regeneration, homeostasis, reproduction, etc.  Generation Lab’s solution is based on peer-reviewed science – including groundbreaking research led by Dr. Irina Conboy, UC Berkeley, and published as a cover story in Aging in Sept 2023.

“Certain molecular disbalance causes degenerative diseases that flare up with aging,” explained Dr. Conboy, co-founder of Generation Lab, and Professor at University of California at Berkeley. “Generation Lab’s tests enable us to identify when a person has a proclivity towards certain conditions and suggest approaches that can help to attenuate, delay or even eliminate those risks. This paves the way to novel anti-aging medicine for identifying and treating diseases early – even when a person is pre-symptomatic – which leads to better outcomes.”

Here’s how Generation Lab works:

* Obtain a test kit from a physician, clinic, or from Generation Lab directly.
* Open the kit and scan the QR code, selecting your level of membership (entry-level subscriptions start at about $400 per year for three tests).
* Follow the directions for a cheek swab and mail the sample back to Generation Lab in a pre-addressed envelope or return it to the clinician who provided the test.
* Within a few weeks, Generation Lab will provide a personalized report highlighting the status of your specific molecular aging markers, and offering a virtual call with a physician to review clinical and lifestyle interventions you might consider to slow the aging process or improve health.
* After subsequent Generation Lab tests, your clinician can review how effective each intervention was and its impact on your health and rate of aging.

Co-founded by [Irina Conboy](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATUU2LHKSbPg854wTzRTSv0e9PJ582xD6DNSESk2gn6QZaexS60B0sE2Zi9kyeK2jSBQ-3D-3DBHg5_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioJD040nHobNbpWz3V5eSPxVO0LZnsm0kHydeleYuGZWPAqLGD-2FWNnuMi78Y3hx-2FJJs7syIuUdGHZgTzLq0IeYHUkTq3kHAojLc41S7LnQF6xFlpjM1CvwfSNSR66Uv5gq7prLU1SDZd7kqOHJAnDKs-3D), a preeminent aging and longevity researcher from UC Berkeley, Generation Lab combines a simple cheek-swab test with proprietary techniques to definitively measure molecular disbalance that indicates aging and risk of disease. The company works with clinicians who can recommend personalized interventions, and Generation Lab can measure the effectiveness of these over time.

According to the [CDC](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATURHuYuDkY-2F8kFuqzju5-2BdQf-2BplA0M-2BpvnD5M2MUQMvg8jjSButV23RpULQLJ66ySbw-3D-3DedM0_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoioyR5HW0MF-2F2k3wNtm38leZlFS8yrqfiu3ULrrxw8OXqO-2B-2FZRj7Zcq7E8zOrI9yqVhDXhwF7MbNUczj9zCLnhvMv0yj1ULZBvLvu2NQFYWgL-2B36K94LDfNISp4AuDz5ggr5YCIh28H2vxG1TnuAhq6M-3D), six in ten adults in the US have at least one chronic disease and four in ten adults have two or more. Many of these are correlated with aging. These diseases are a major cause of morbidity and mortality and place a significant burden on our healthcare system. Catching these conditions early and preventing their progression can save millions of lives and billions of dollars. That’s what Generation Lab plans to do.

Unlike biological clocks, which are based on linear models that predict aging and disease by comparing a few people to each other, Generation Lab quantifies the actual biological age of each person through precise measurement of molecular biological noise – a key barometer of aging. Moreover, the biological age is quantified by Generation Lab for each function of the body: inflammation, regeneration, homeostasis, reproduction, etc.  Generation Lab’s solution is based on peer-reviewed science – including groundbreaking research led by Dr. Irina Conboy, UC Berkeley, and published as a cover story in Aging in Sept 2023.

“Certain molecular disbalance causes degenerative diseases that flare up with aging,” explained Dr. Conboy, co-founder of Generation Lab, and Professor at University of California at Berkeley. “Generation Lab’s tests enable us to identify when a person has a proclivity towards certain conditions and suggest approaches that can help to attenuate, delay or even eliminate those risks. This paves the way to novel anti-aging medicine for identifying and treating diseases early – even when a person is pre-symptomatic – which leads to better outcomes.”

[George Church, Ph. D](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATUdXTV3iQG28JGooOhy7cWzK92SocBokvhLFK6sB33ZQgpoi7p8Qlun30qw4VYNguOZoVNWv6gPcBqcnbfXxbMRQ-3DYiY0_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4Joig4aeR72jOsxLtXKgAH76cI7dD1E-2FZo0r1b6sRHAf2grux18hcZ2XVWT9FthMeqfDyOGrDFUD7fMo6e2TnIKHNV45JCbtvf99DMKv9tdHE2W-2BAX5jlWzc6OiyDQjW-2FqpygTXEBo0Zo4y1TxIb-2BuBq4A-3D)., the Robert Winthrop Professor of Genetics at Harvard Medical School, is a renowned geneticist who is serving as an advisor to Generation Lab. “Irina and her team are taking cutting edge research straight out of the lab and applying it to a pressing issue: identifying disease much earlier than ever before possible using genetic markers,” said Dr. Church. “Seeing this unfold, and having a front-row seat to its impact, is going to be a pivotal moment in epigenetics – it’s incredibly exciting.”

Here’s how Generation Lab works:

Generation Lab’s other two co-founders are [Alina Rui Su](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATUegha2L32XA5BGAQD-2FrrWT8ik1GwoEXnvGpGYc2nrM18TU4N_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoisM8XpuULqO1EOlO7whnQKgDfuutkYV8iNDIuuXaaNxIeNiGySaHts-2BOY8mibNTnXhhYJCWb7WQnV3eicUCcIvQwY3p7ss-2FZQ-2Ba24xxu0LyMjFwIUhcJgZbik2E27w-2BRVSK-2B1hm-2FXk2f5LggQRB-2Fmco-3D) and [Michael Suswal](https://u7061146.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=4tNED-2FM8iDZJQyQ53jATUegha2L32XA5BGAQD-2FrrWT-2FuMsN-2FvBofucFSDTFfKWPfrn49-2FbNt8DeQtyIvnpeQDQ-3D-3DOLIN_TIUcMwzJEyy2Sf9Tw0-2BD1ji7lvoeVo1QHbCfgo5PwaA0QxzO1GSTLMu09Mmf36or1WRiNw1uy3cyqQamaEM-2BawiUZevxu8GNBA-2Fbb6dyRppH2RgJvthLEAPbbppMmnlMEBqhRGhU4KQmD9q0OU3QBtqb07U1ebc0SwO1MOYd0XuDZ1UL7zQPR2MOEMUdzyStYiCuqYTDrGAk4gC9B4JoiiOiLgj4laIyYQqUlrv6DFacKhLUkeM5u-2F-2BpJ1fm5-2FlekiR26YXUQZrwohjCU31Bd0GNhCRJ1X4Pd7PXaPLnjpaPStkqp-2Bpu0PstT0ZkUWQtWMGyl3OH-2BHbDGn0iF4UWBKpAogFdrSujYkO3lSc-2FmRQ-3D). Su, who studied longevity at Harvard Medical School and Berkeley, was previously founder and CEO of NovaXS. She serves as Generation Lab’s CEO, setting the vision and leading the company’s development. Suswal, who was previously co-founder and COO of tech startup Standard AI (where he remains on the Board), serves as COO, leading Generation Lab’s operations and commercialization efforts.

“As rejuvenation research expands, we need to focus on identifying the most effective measurement for aging. My passion in healthcare led me to focus on Aging & Regeneration since I was a researcher in Irina’s lab at Berkeley,” said Su. “Generation Lab’s technology has the potential to impact an 8-billion-person global population. We will be a one-stop shop for testing plus clinician recommendations for interventions with the ability to ship personalized products to your doorstep. I’m proud that we’re making it easy for people to take a more proactive role in their healthcare and better understand their risk of aging factors.”

Generation Labs has raised a pre-seed round from Transpose Platform and Sequoia China Founding Partner. The company expects its aging test kits to be available in Q1 2024. To join the waitlist, visit [generationlab.co](http://generationlab.co/).

Our take on 2025: It was the Year Data Became the Bottleneck for AI
If 2024 was the year AI captured imagination, 2025 was the year reality caught up.
Across enterprise conversations this year, one pattern showed up everywhere:
AI is moving fast. Data is not.
In this end-of-year discussion, we reflect on what we actually saw inside large organizations—past the demos and hype cycles:
• Strong belief in AI, real budgets opening
• AI teams racing ahead in isolation
• Data teams stuck maintaining brittle stacks and pipelines
• Business teams frustrated by slow progress
The surprise wasn’t model limitations.
It was how often the basics still aren’t in place for humans, let alone agents: discovery, access, trust, governance.
In some cases, AI is paused or banned—not due to skepticism, but because the blast radius of robots acting on untrusted data is simply too high.
This is why incremental fixes to the modern data stack aren’t enough. The gap between app development speed and data readiness is now orders of magnitude wide—and widening.
That’s the shift we describe as Data 3.0: autonomous data products, governance by default, and data designed for AI consumption—not retrofitted for it.
We also talk candidly about:
• Why governance (when automated) is finally becoming an enabler
• Why “AI-ready data” is everywhere—and still poorly defined
• What leaders should actually focus on in 2026 (hint: speed from data → action)
If you’re a data, AI, or technology leader feeling this tension, this conversation will likely sound familiar.

Scientifically, meta-analysis is extremely useful for estimating the general effect of a particular intervention across a wide range of contexts.
I am less convinced, however, of its "direct" usefulness for practitioners.
First, most implementation sites are not “average” sites. They deviate from the mean in systematic ways.
Second, and more fundamentally, practitioners’ key problem is not learning the average effect size, but learning what can be inferred from evidence generated in one setting (i.e., a source site) for a different, target setting. This is what Eugene Bardach (2004) termed the “extrapolation problem” in his APPAM presidential address, where he offered a set of practical heuristics for translating evidence across contexts.
If I had time and resources, I would love to build and field-test an LLM-based agentic tool that explicitly requires structured information about both the source and target sites (based on Bardach and others' ideas), and then generates an implementation roadmap that highlights adaptation needs, risks, and capacity-building requirements.
For scaling evidence in practice, we may need tools like this. What has changed is that it is now substantially less costly to develop flexible and adaptive analytic tools that take contextual heterogeneity seriously.
Please let me know if you have come across prototypes or other examples based on a similar idea. I would love to learn from them.

Synthetic data is quietly reshaping market research, and its potential is impressive. Its ability to generate plausible, human-like datasets at scale opens new ways to explore insights, preserve privacy, and stress-test assumptions. In many ways, it feels like a real game-changer.
At the same time, I can't deny the subtle philosophical tension that's been lurking in the background. What happens to the purpose of market research when listening to real people can, in some cases, be replaced by models of them? What do we risk losing in nuance, in surprise, and in the small frictions that often produce the most interesting insights?
I explore these questions in a short reflective essay about how synthetic data interacts with the core role of market research as a practice of listening rather than just modelling, and highlight some of the quiet questions we should keep in mind to make sure we use it wisely.
If you are curious about the balance between the promise of synthetic data and its deeper implications, you can read the essay here:

Data doesn’t just tell us what happened anymore. It’s starting to whisper what’s about to happen next. ❄️
The 11th Edition of the Data-powered Innovation Review is here, and it explores how we’re stepping into the age of anticipation, where technology senses earlier, acts sooner, and supports a society with greater agency, autonomy, and confidence than ever before.
On page 22, we introduced a leitmotif around Space Data as a Service, centered on a society empowered by technology that is everywhere, senses the world with growing precision, and acts earlier with more agency, autonomy, and authority than ever before. This edition revisits that idea, reflecting on what we observed two years ago and how the notion of anticipation is taking shape today.
As we continue to explore space, the amount of space data we collect will continue to grow. This data will allow us to make new discoveries about the universe and develop new technologies that will benefit humanity.

Cytolytics•3K followers

I often hear from teams that clustering tools create as many challenges as they solve—especially when it comes to interpreting results or sinking hours into [#annotating](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fannotating&trk=public_post-text) algorithm-derived populations.
At Cytolution, we set out to remove that analysis paralysis.
Our approach is intentionally simple: just define a few basic gates for the cell types you care about. There’s no need to build a full gating strategy upfront. We automatically turn those gates into a clear, fully annotated Population Tree — human-interpretable from the very start.
From there, our algorithms help you expand that initial simple gating, guiding deeper exploration without adding complexity. The outcome: meaningful, trustworthy populations without endless manual annotation or guesswork.
I’m incredibly proud of what the team has built—and excited to see how much time (and frustration😄) it will save researchers and how it may lead to yet undiscovered paths.

Introducing cRhona: An AI co-pilot for rapid evidence synthesis in policy making
How can AI responsibly accelerate evidence-informed policymaking? At [Center for Rapid Evidence Synthesis (ACRES)](https://ug.linkedin.com/company/acres-rrs?trk=public_post-text), we've built cRhona to find out.
cRhona is an AI platform designed to amplify—not replace—the critical human expertise in our established Rapid Response Service (RRS). It 'clones' the core functions of an evidence intermediary: coordinating requests, searches, synthesis, and collaborative review while ensuring accuracy, transparency, and team coordination.
The result? Policymakers, evidence intermediaries, and domain experts can co-produce rapid, high-quality evidence summaries faster than ever—including for decisions requiring evidence in under 48 hours.
This tool digitises our proven RRS model, which has served Uganda's health, education, energy, and gender sectors by delivering demand-driven, synthesised evidence in accessible packages.
We believe AI can be a powerful force multiplier in the race to connect vital knowledge with urgent action.

To solve this, Accion Labs built a GenAI-enabled Data Agent for an international health organization. Teams can now retrieve insights from structured databases simply by asking questions in natural language.

✅75% reduction in turnaround time
✅Faster program planning and evaluations
✅Reduced dependency on technical teams
✅A secure, cloud-ready interface fit for global scale

My career moves between two…•2K followers

You can have the fastest processing and the best predictions. But if your model fails when it meets a patient from Latin America because it considers them an "outlier," your technology has hit a ceiling.
An outlier is not an error. It’s a person whose data (genetic, clinical, and environmental) was missing from your training phase.
Latin America isn't an anomaly. It is a critical piece of the global puzzle. For AI to reach its full potential, it requires real, traceable, and structured data from diverse populations.
This is our mission at [Cromodata](https://ar.linkedin.com/company/cromodata?trk=public_post-text): Elevating the standard of Latin American health data to ensure global science leaves no one behind.
